title: Ezekiel 17
subtitle: The Two Eagles and the Vine

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Call of Ezekiel

sections.header: Verses 1-10 — The Great Eagle, the Cedar Top, and the Vine

heb.paragraph: God calls this oracle a hidah (riddle) and a mashal (parable) — a deliberately encoded story requiring interpretation. The first eagle is Nebuchadnezzar, the cedar top is Jehoiachin, the seed planted in fertile soil is Zedekiah, and the second eagle is Pharaoh of Egypt. The riddle format engages the audience before exposing their king's treachery.

refs.note: Nebuchadnezzar installed Zedekiah as king — the historical event behind the 'planting' of the vine.
Zedekiah's appeal to Egypt and Pharaoh's brief military approach — the second eagle in action.
Zedekiah rebelled against Nebuchadnezzar who had made him swear by God — oath-breaking as covenant violation.

notes.note: The great eagle with powerful wings and rich plumage is Nebuchadnezzar. He took the top of the cedar (Jehoiachin) and carried it to a city of merchants (Babylon).
The seed planted in fertile soil that became a spreading vine is Zedekiah — placed on the throne with every opportunity to thrive under Babylonian vassalage.
The vine reached toward the second eagle (Egypt) despite being planted in good soil with abundant water. Zedekiah's rebellion was foolish as well as faithless.
Will it thrive? It will be uprooted — no great force needed. The east wind (Babylon) will wither it completely.

Calvin reads the parable as a lesson in political theology: vassals who break oaths, even oaths to pagan kings, violate God's moral order. Political pragmatism that overrides covenant faithfulness invites divine judgment.
The 'great eagle' (nesher gadol) is the griffon vulture rather than the modern eagle — the largest bird of prey in the ancient Near East and a royal symbol in Mesopotamian art.
The 'city of merchants' (ir rekullah) is Babylon, a major commercial center. The cedar top (Jehoiachin) was taken there in 597 BC.

Block identifies the genre as a political fable (Tierfabel) combined with plant imagery — a complex literary form that works on two levels simultaneously. The surface story of eagles and vines encodes the geopolitical reality of Babylonian-Egyptian rivalry with Judah caught between.

Zimmerli stresses that the riddle format is not merely literary decoration but functions to force the audience to participate in their own judgment. By solving the riddle, they condemn the vine's disloyalty — and then discover they are the vine.

hist.context: The allegory maps precisely onto recent history: Nebuchadnezzar (the first great eagle) took the top of the cedar (King Jehoiachin) to Babylon and planted a vine (Zedekiah) in the land. But the vine turned its roots toward a second eagle (Egypt), seeking water (military alliance). The question: will the vine prosper after betraying the one who planted it? The answer: no — it will be uprooted.

sections.header: Verses 11-24 — Oath-Breaking and the Cosmic Cedar

heb.paragraph: Zedekiah's oath to Nebuchadnezzar was sworn in God's name — making the political treaty a sacred covenant. Breaking the oath is not merely political treachery but violation of God's name. God treats pagan treaty oaths as binding when sworn in his name, placing the integrity of his own reputation above Judah's political interests.

refs.note: A shoot from the stump of Jesse — Isaiah's parallel messianic image of a tender branch from the royal line.
Nebuchadnezzar's dream of a great tree sheltering all creatures — Daniel's cosmic tree parallels Ezekiel's cosmic cedar.
The mustard seed growing into a great tree where birds nest — Jesus uses the same cosmic tree imagery.
The kingdom of God is like a mustard seed — the smallest becoming the greatest, echoing the tender shoot.

notes.note: The oath broken was sworn in God's name. Zedekiah's treachery against Babylon was simultaneously treachery against God. Breaking a divine oath invokes the covenant curse.
The tender shoot planted on the mountain is messianic — God himself will establish the Davidic king that human politics failed to produce.

Calvin sees the cosmic cedar as Christ: the tender shoot from the high cedar is the Messiah from David's line, planted by God on Zion, growing to shelter all nations. Human kings fail; God's King endures.
Zedekiah will die in Babylon — a prediction fulfilled when he was blinded and taken there (2 Kgs 25:7), where he presumably died in captivity.
The 'high mountain of Israel' is Zion — the cosmic mountain tradition where God's kingdom extends over all nations.

Block reads the cosmic cedar oracle as Ezekiel's contribution to messianic theology: God reverses the eagles' work. Where Nebuchadnezzar plucked the cedar top, God plants a tender shoot. Where human empires uproot and transplant for political control, God plants for universal shelter.

Zimmerli sees the reversal formula ('I bring low the high tree and make high the low tree') as eschatological — God overturns all human political orders. The cosmic cedar is not merely a better king but a new order of reality.

hist.context: The interpretation (vv.11-21) is followed by a messianic oracle (vv.22-24) that transforms the entire allegory. God himself will take a tender shoot from the top of the cedar (the Davidic line) and plant it on a high mountain (Zion), where it will grow into a magnificent cedar sheltering all nations. The political failure of human kings (Zedekiah) will be replaced by God's own planting — the messianic king.

rows.label: vv.1-10
rows.text: The Riddle: Two Eagles and a Vine

lit.note: Political fable becomes messianic oracle: human kings fail through treachery, but God plants his own king — a tender shoot that becomes the cosmic cedar sheltering all nations.

themes.note: Covenant at 9: oath-breaking is the central offense, and God's planting of the cosmic cedar is covenant restoration. Sovereignty at 9: God overrules human politics with his own planting.

The riddle format engages the audience before exposing their king's treachery.

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Two Eagles and the Vine

translations.text: Say to them, ‘This is what the Sovereign Lord says: A great eagle with powerful wings, long feathers and full plumage of varied colors came to Lebanon. Taking hold of the top of a cedar,
say, Thus says the Lord GOD: A great eagle with great wings and long pinions, rich in plumage of many colors, came to Lebanon and took the top of the cedar.
“ ‘He took one of the seedlings of the land and put it in fertile soil. He planted it like a willow by abundant water,
Then he took of the seed of the land and planted it in fertile soil. He placed it beside abundant waters. He set it like a willow twig,
